ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic chicken curry concept (FOODON_03315456) but does not have a Burmese-specific curry entry. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken curry — closest parent used for Burmese-style preparation with shallots, turmeric, fish sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a Burmese-specific chicken curry code. A0ACQ (chicken curry) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for Burmese chicken curry exists. SNOMED CT food coverage for Southeast Asian ethnic dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Burmese Chicken Curry is approximately 250g, comprising bone-in chicken pieces with sauce, typically eaten alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia — Chicken curry, Burmese style
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020 contains a direct entry for Burmese chicken curry (Kyat Tha Hin). Values are taken directly from this entry. Confidence is high.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimation for Burmese Chicken Curry — analogous low-carbohydrate meat curry estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: Burmese Chicken Curry contains only ~3.2g carbohydrate per 100g (8g per 250g serving), primarily from shallots, garlic, and tomato. No direct GI test exists for this dish. GI ~15 is estimated by analogy with low-carbohydrate meat-based curries and stews in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI values of 10–20 due to negligible available carbohydrate. GL per 250g serving = (15 × 8) / 100 = 1.2, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from fish sauce — approximately 750mg per 250g serving (37.5% of 2000mg daily limit)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should be aware that a single 250g serving of Burmese Chicken Curry contributes approximately 750mg sodium (37.5% of the 2000mg daily limit), primarily from fish sauce; portion control and requesting reduced fish sauce when dining out are practical strategies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and negligible glycemic load (~1.2 per serving) — clinical concern is from co-consumed rice, not the curry itself
type2_diabetes.guidance: Burmese Chicken Curry has a GI of approximately 15 and a GL of only 1.2 per 250g serving, making it safe for people with Type 2 Diabetes; the 28g protein and 20g fat per serving slow gastric emptying and moderate postprandial glucose when eaten alongside rice, and consuming the curry before the rice is a practical meal-sequencing strategy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~6.25g per 250g serving) and high sodium (~750mg per serving) from cooking oil and chicken skin; predominantly MUFA-rich groundnut oil is a favourable factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, Burmese Chicken Curry provides approximately 6.25g saturated fat and 750mg sodium per 250g serving; the predominant cooking fat is groundnut oil (rich in monounsaturated oleic acid, ~50%), which is cardioprotective, but total fat intake (~20g per serving) and sodium should be monitored in the context of overall daily intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) and MUFA-rich cooking oil
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric (~30mg curcumin per serving) and ginger (1–2g equivalent per serving) in Burmese Chicken Curry have documented anti-inflammatory properties, and the predominantly MUFA-rich groundnut oil is favourable for inflammation, though curcumin dose is below therapeutic threshold.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burmese Chicken Curry (ကြက်သားဟင်း, romanized: Kyat Tha Hin) is a traditional Myanmar home-cooked dish consisting of bone-in chicken pieces simmered in a paste of shallots, garlic, and ginger, seasoned with turmeric, dried chilli, and fish sauce. Unlike Indian curries, Burmese curry uses minimal spice complexity — no garam masala or complex spice blends — and relies on fish sauce as the primary seasoning rather than salt. A defining cultural characteristic is the intentional pooling of oil on the surface, which is considered a quality indicator in Burmese culinary tradition. The curry has a rich, savoury, mildly spiced flavour with a pronounced umami depth from fish sauce. Per 250g serving (without rice): approximately 390 kcal, 28g protein, 20g fat (predominantly from groundnut oil and chicken skin), 8g carbohydrate, 750mg sodium. GI is approximately 15 (negligible carbohydrate), GL approximately 1.2 — the dish itself has minimal glycemic impact. It is invariably served with steamed white rice in Myanmar cuisine. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content. The cooking oil is predominantly groundnut oil (MUFA-rich), which is favourable for cardiovascular health. Sodium is the primary clinical concern due to fish sauce. Available at Burmese restaurants in Singapore and throughout Southeast Asia. FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020 direct entry.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020, which contains a specific entry for Burmese chicken curry (Kyat Tha Hin). This is a primary regional food composition database approved under Project Veritas source policy. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology for low-carbohydrate meat curries, clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Burmese chicken curry is not separately reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020 entry; USDA FoodData Central does not contain a Burmese chicken curry entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020 entry for this dish. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020 (no industrial trans fats in traditional preparation). All core macros (protein_g 11.2, fat_g 8.0, carbohydrate_g 3.2) are non-zero and well-supported. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database methodology; no direct GI test exists for Burmese chicken curry. The dish is always consumed with steamed rice in practice; the GI and GL values reported here are for the curry alone (without rice). Sodium of 750mg per 250g serving (300mg per 100g) is the primary clinical concern. The visible oil layer on the surface is a traditional Burmese quality indicator and is intentional.
